g.data-narration: Classes in C++ are the foundation of Object-Oriented Programming, enabling encapsulation of data and functions.
A class is a blueprint that bundles data members and member functions together, unlike C structures.
g.text: A user-defined data type that encapsulates

g.data-narration: Classes enable encapsulation, data hiding, and modular code design essential for OOP in C++.
The class keyword declares a new type with access specifiers controlling member visibility.
Private members are hidden, public members form the interface, and protected members are for inheritance.
Data members store object state; member functions define behavior. Member functions can be const.
Objects are instances of classes. Each object has its own data but shares member function code.
g.text: Stack: Rectangle rect1; Dynamic: Rectangle* p = new Rectangle(10,5);

g.data-narration: Constructors initialize objects automatically. They have the same name as the class and no return type.
g.text: Box(int w, int h) { width=w; height=h; }
✦ Same name as class ✦ No return type ✦ Called automatically on object creation

g.data-narration: Multiple constructors with different parameters enable flexible object creation - this is constructor overloading.
Destructors release resources when objects are destroyed. They have ~ prefix and no return type or parameters.
g.text: Same name as class with ~ prefix
No return type, no parameters • Auto-called on destruction

g.data-narration: The this pointer references the current object. It's useful for distinguishing parameters from data members.
g.text: this->id = id; // this points to current object
Implicit pointer available in all non-static
member functions • Points to the calling object

g.data-narration: Static members belong to the class, not objects. One copy is shared by all instances.
g.text: ✦ Must be defined outside class ✦ Shared by all objects ✦ Accessed via ClassName::member

g.data-narration: Friend functions access private members without being members. They're declared with the friend keyword.
Copy constructor creates new objects; assignment operator assigns to existing objects. Know the difference!
g.text: MyClass obj2 = obj1; // Creates NEW object
Called when: new object created from existing • Passed by value • Returns by value

g.data-narration: Key points: classes bundle data and functions, access specifiers control visibility, constructors initialize, destructors clean up.
Remember: constructors have no return type, static members need external definition, private members are never accessible in derived classes.
g.text: ✦ Class members are private by default (struct: public)
✦ Static members MUST be defined outside
✦ Private members inaccessible in derived classes
"Classes enable modular, maintainable, and scalable software design in C++"
